Adverse selection significance test — markout at t=300s, H₁: mean < 0 (one-tailed t-test). Bands use naive SE (std/√n); CIs may be too narrow if fills within the same ticker are correlated.
Price Bucket n fills Mean markout t-stat p-value Significance
0–20¢ 3,411 +3.23¢ 16.91 1.000 ns
20–40¢ 2,109 +1.98¢ 7.65 1.000 ns
40–60¢ 2,153 +1.99¢ 7.75 1.000 ns
60–80¢ 1,485 +2.19¢ 5.93 1.000 ns
80–100¢ 1,627 +3.88¢ 11.57 1.000 ns

Markout measured from fill price. At t=0 the value equals EV captured (fill price vs microprice). A declining curve indicates adverse selection eroding edge over time. Red dashed line = breakeven. Buy = bought YES (long); Sell = sold YES (short).

Price bucket chart: fills grouped by the price they were filled at. Shaded bands = 95% CI (naive SE = std/√n). Near-extreme buckets (0–20¢, 80–100¢) with steeply declining curves indicate adverse selection. Significance table tests H₁: mean markout < 0 at the final horizon. CIs may be too narrow if multiple fills on the same ticker are correlated.

Fills within the same event (e.g. multiple spread brackets on one basketball game) are correlated — their markouts move together. The naive SE (std/√N) treats fills as independent and overstates statistical significance. The cluster-robust SE groups fills by event and accounts for within-event correlation. The design effect (cluster SE / naive SE) shows how much correlation inflates uncertainty; values > 2 mean the effective sample size is < 25% of the raw fill count. If the worst-markout events are concentrated in a few correlated clusters, the session-wide markout may be driven by one or two bad outcomes rather than a systematic edge problem.

3¢4,393+0.3511¢0
4+¢20,787+0.8608¢0

Only REPLACE and CANCEL decisions are logged (KEEPs are omitted for storage efficiency). Amend API call count from the latency table serves as a cross-check for REPLACE count. Rapid oscillations (multiple REPLACEs within 5s on the same ticker+side) indicate the microprice is jittering around a price boundary. Queue Ahead Lost shows the queue position sacrificed when moving — high values on small price moves suggest the fill model undervalues queue advantage.

1000+443,7900.00170.00590.00000.0083

Sampled at 1:200 from every estimate_fill_probability() call. Context: keep_ev = evaluating a resting order's fill chance with queue advantage; move_ev = estimating fill at a new price (back of queue); quote_sweep = initial quote price search. If keep_ev and move_ev show similar fill probabilities despite very different contracts_ahead, the model is not sensitive enough to queue depth.
